{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11645813]]
Wikidata name: wild onsen
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:22, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{afc comment|1=Here is an attempt

* 野湯 (Wilderness Hot Springs):  
  These are naturally occurring, undeveloped hot springs found “in the wild.” They lack formal commercial facilities and often require visitors to set up their own bathing arrangements or rely on minimal local upkeep.

* 秘湯 (Secret Hot Springs):  
  Coined around the mid‑1970s, this term refers to hot springs tucked away in remote, hard-to-reach areas (often deep in the mountains). They evoke an aura of mystery and tradition, appealing to those seeking a quiet, off‑the‑beaten‑path retreat.

* 隠し湯 (Hidden Hot Springs):  
  Historically linked to feudal times, these were hot springs that local lords or elites kept exclusively for their own use, deliberately concealed from the public. Today, the term emphasizes their exclusive, almost “forbidden” allure.

* 引湯 (Drawn‑off Hot Springs):  
  Unlike the others that are used at the site of emergence, drawn‑off hot springs involve hot water being piped from its natural source to another location. This process often cools the water slightly and is used to distribute onsen water to developed bathing facilities.

Each term reflects a different aspect—whether it’s the natural setting, historical secrecy, or the method of utilization.

[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:59, 25 March 2025 (UTC)}}
{{afc comment|1=Need to figure out how to properly differentiate these four types
*{{ill|Wild hot spring|ja|野湯|1=Wild Hot Springs|1=Wild Hot Springs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wild hot spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Wild Hot Springs|qq=}}
*{{ill|Secret hot spring|ja|秘湯|1=Secret hot spring|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Secret hot spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Secret hot spring|qq=}}
*{{ill|Hidden hot spring|ja|隠し湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hidden hot spring|qq=}}
*{{ill|Drawn‐off hot spring|ja|引湯|1=Drawn‐off hot spring|2=ja|3=引湯|lt=Drawn‐off hot spring|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Drawn‐off hot spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Drawn‐off hot spring|qq=}}
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:50, 25 March 2025 (UTC)}}
{{More citations needed}}
'''Noyu''' (野湯) refers to '''hot springs''' that emerge '''naturally in the wild''' without any commercial facilities built around their source. These hot springs exist in various forms—some are maintained regularly by local community associations or enthusiasts, while others require visitors to bring materials such as sheets, sandbags, or shovels to prepare a bathing spot. Some *noyu* pose life-threatening risks due to volcanic gas emissions like '''hydrogen sulfide''', while others are off-limits for reasons such as '''environmental protection''' or '''private ownership restrictions'''.

== Natural hot springs flowing from the source ==  

[[File:Nakadake_hotspring_daisetuzan01.jpg|right|thumb|{{ill|Nakadake Onsen|ja|中岳温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakadake Onsen|qq=}} open-air bath, with a maintenance shovel visible]]
The most common form of *noyu* involves hot springs naturally emerging along riverbanks or mountain slopes. In dormant and active volcanoes, groundwater is heated by magma and erupts to form hot spring rivers or even entire '''thermal lakes'''.

=== The hot spring gushes forth from the mountainside ===  

This type occurs when '''hot spring sources seep from mountain slopes''' or, in geothermal areas, '''multiple springs burst out of the ground'''. These sites often lack built-in bathing facilities, requiring visitors to dig holes in the ground or use portable plastic pools or sheets to create makeshift baths. In some cases, volunteers construct basic bath structures, but {{Citation needed|some of these may have been built without landowner permission or illegally within national parks.|date=January 2011}}. Temperature control is often difficult.

Examples: {{ill|Nakadake Onsen|ja|中岳温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakadake Onsen|qq=}}

=== A hot spring gushes forth from the riverbank ===  

[[File:Hotspring_Kawayu02.jpg|right|thumb|{{ill|Kawayu Onsen (Wakayama)|ja|川湯温泉 (和歌山県)|lt=Kawayu Onsen|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawayu Onsen (Wakayama)|1=Kawayu onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11478936}} Senninburo, with a bath visible across the river]]
In these cases, digging into a '''riverbed''' releases hot spring water. This type allows for '''easy temperature control''' using river water, making it a popular form of *noyu*.

Examples:  
- {{ill|Kawayu Onsen (Wakayama)|ja|川湯温泉 (和歌山県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawayu Onsen (Wakayama)|1=Kawayu onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11478936}}  
- {{ill|Kamiyu Onsen|ja|上湯温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamiyu Onsen|1=Kamiyu Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11359605}}  
- {{ill|Kiriake Onsen|ja|切明温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiriake Onsen|1=Kiriake Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11396212}}  

=== The rivers and lakes themselves are hot springs ===  

This category includes '''rivers and lakes where the bottom serves as a hot spring source'''. If the water is sufficiently hot or the spring has a large flow rate, the entire body of water can become '''suitable for bathing'''.

Examples:  
- {{ill|Kawarage Ōyu Falls|ja|川原毛大湯滝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawarage Ōyu Falls|1=Kawarageoyu Falls|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11477876}}  
- {{ill|Kamuiwakka Falls|ja|カムイワッカの滝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamuiwakka Falls|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カムイワッカ湯の滝|1=Kamuiwakka Falls|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6359812}}  
- {{ill|Ikenoyu (Teshikaga Town)|ja|池の湯 (弟子屈町)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikenoyu (Teshikaga Town)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}  
- {{ill|Yunokawa (Noboribetsu Onsen)|ja|登別温泉 湯の川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yunokawa (Noboribetsu Onsen)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}  
- {{ill|Iōtorino-yu|ja|秋田焼山 硫黄取りの湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iōtorino-yu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}  
- {{ill|Shiriyaki Onsen|ja|尻焼温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiriyaki Onsen|1=Shiriyaki Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11464890}}  
- {{ill|Shinyu (Tateyama Caldera)|ja|立山カルデラ 新湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinyu (Tateyama Caldera)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}  

=== The tide pools on the coast are hot springs ===  

{{ill|Higashi Onsen|ja|東温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi Onsen|1=Higashi Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11527997}}
[[File:Iwojima_Higashi_onsen.jpg|thumb|Hot spring at *Higashi Onsen* on Satsuma Iōjima, overlooking the East China Sea]]
Some '''coastal tide pools''' act as natural '''hot springs'''. They are submerged during '''high tide''' and only accessible during '''low tide'''. In some cases, '''waves have carved caves into rocky cliffs''', forming hidden hot spring pools.

Examples:  
- {{ill|Jinata Onsen|ja|地鉈温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinata Onsen|1=Jinata Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11424716}}  
- {{ill|Mizunashi Kaigan Onsen|ja|水無海浜温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizunashi Kaigan Onsen|1=Mizunashi-Kaihin Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11548954}}  

== Anthropogenic discharge ==  

Sometimes, '''construction projects accidentally uncover hot springs''', such as '''roadwork''' or '''foundation digging''' that breaches underground thermal sources. In some cases, municipalities construct '''public bathing areas''' beside these sites, but in others, the water is left to '''flow freely''', forming *noyu* that bathers can use. Some abandoned '''mining sites''' also feature hot springs that emerged from excavation activities.

Examples:  
- {{ill|Kinka-yu|ja|金花湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinka-yu|1=Kinkatou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11647826}}  
- {{ill|Oku-oku Hachikuro Onsen|ja|奥奥八九郎温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oku-oku Hachikuro Onsen|1=Oku-oku Hachikuro Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17224546}}  

== A place that was once a hot spring resort ==  

These were once '''established hot spring resorts''', but due to factors such as '''poor accessibility''' or '''economic decline''', they were abandoned. Some still have intact '''bathtubs''', making them '''usable as wild hot springs'''.

Examples:  
- {{ill|Kōso Onsen|ja|香草温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōso Onsen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}  
- {{ill|Goshiki-yu Ruins|ja|五色湯跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goshiki-yu Ruins|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}  
- {{ill|Kawakita Onsen|ja|川北温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawakita Onsen|1=Kawakita Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11477827}}  
- {{ill|Mitata Onsen|ja|見立温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mitata Onsen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}  

== Use of well-maintained hot springs ==  

Some *noyu* '''reuse discarded thermal water''' from developed '''hot spring resorts'''. In many cases, volunteers '''construct informal bathing facilities''' at these sites. In the past, '''Matsuzaki Onsen''' had an open-air bath created beneath a spring well that collected overflowed hot water. 

Examples:  
- {{ill|Yunotani Onsen|ja|湯の谷温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yunotani Onsen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}  
- {{ill|Kawazu-hama Onsen|ja|河津浜温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawazu-hama Onsen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}

*{{ill|Secret hot spring|ja|秘湯|1=Secret hot spring|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Secret hot spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Secret hot spring|qq=}}
*{{ill|Hidden hot spring|ja|隠し湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hidden hot spring|qq=}}
*{{ill|Drawn‐off hot spring|ja|引湯|1=Drawn‐off hot spring|2=ja|3=引湯|lt=Drawn‐off hot spring|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Drawn‐off hot spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Drawn‐off hot spring|qq=}}
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:50, 25 March 2025 (UTC)}}
{{More citations needed}}
'''Noyu''' (野湯) refers to '''hot springs''' that emerge '''naturally in the wild''' without any commercial facilities built around their source. These hot springs exist in various forms—some are maintained regularly by local community associations or enthusiasts, while others require visitors to bring materials such as sheets, sandbags, or shovels to prepare a bathing spot. Some *noyu* pose life-threatening risks due to volcanic gas emissions like '''hydrogen sulfide''', while others are off-limits for reasons such as '''environmental protection''' or '''private ownership restrictions'''.

== Natural hot springs flowing from the source ==  

[[File:Nakadake_hotspring_daisetuzan01.jpg|right|thumb|{{ill|Nakadake Onsen|ja|中岳温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakadake Onsen|qq=}} open-air bath, with a maintenance shovel visible]]
The most common form of *noyu* involves hot springs naturally emerging along riverbanks or mountain slopes. In dormant and active volcanoes, groundwater is heated by magma and erupts to form hot spring rivers or even entire '''thermal lakes'''.

=== The hot spring gushes forth from the mountainside ===  

This type occurs when '''hot spring sources seep from mountain slopes''' or, in geothermal areas, '''multiple springs burst out of the ground'''. These sites often lack built-in bathing facilities, requiring visitors to dig holes in the ground or use portable plastic pools or sheets to create makeshift baths. In some cases, volunteers construct basic bath structures, but {{Citation needed|some of these may have been built without landowner permission or illegally within national parks.|date=January 2011}}. Temperature control is often difficult.

Examples: {{ill|Nakadake Onsen|ja|中岳温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakadake Onsen|qq=}}

=== A hot spring gushes forth from the riverbank ===  

[[File:Hotspring_Kawayu02.jpg|right|thumb|{{ill|Kawayu Onsen (Wakayama)|ja|川湯温泉 (和歌山県)|lt=Kawayu Onsen|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawayu Onsen (Wakayama)|1=Kawayu onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11478936}} Senninburo, with a bath visible across the river]]
In these cases, digging into a '''riverbed''' releases hot spring water. This type allows for '''easy temperature control''' using river water, making it a popular form of *noyu*.

Examples:  
- {{ill|Kawayu Onsen (Wakayama)|ja|川湯温泉 (和歌山県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawayu Onsen (Wakayama)|1=Kawayu onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11478936}}  
- {{ill|Kamiyu Onsen|ja|上湯温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamiyu Onsen|1=Kamiyu Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11359605}}  
- {{ill|Kiriake Onsen|ja|切明温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiriake Onsen|1=Kiriake Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11396212}}  

=== The rivers and lakes themselves are hot springs ===  

This category includes '''rivers and lakes where the bottom serves as a hot spring source'''. If the water is sufficiently hot or the spring has a large flow rate, the entire body of water can become '''suitable for bathing'''.

Examples:  
- {{ill|Kawarage Ōyu Falls|ja|川原毛大湯滝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawarage Ōyu Falls|1=Kawarageoyu Falls|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11477876}}  
- {{ill|Kamuiwakka Falls|ja|カムイワッカの滝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamuiwakka Falls|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カムイワッカ湯の滝|1=Kamuiwakka Falls|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6359812}}  
- {{ill|Ikenoyu (Teshikaga Town)|ja|池の湯 (弟子屈町)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikenoyu (Teshikaga Town)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}  
- {{ill|Yunokawa (Noboribetsu Onsen)|ja|登別温泉 湯の川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yunokawa (Noboribetsu Onsen)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}  
- {{ill|Iōtorino-yu|ja|秋田焼山 硫黄取りの湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iōtorino-yu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}  
- {{ill|Shiriyaki Onsen|ja|尻焼温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiriyaki Onsen|1=Shiriyaki Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11464890}}  
- {{ill|Shinyu (Tateyama Caldera)|ja|立山カルデラ 新湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinyu (Tateyama Caldera)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}  

=== The tide pools on the coast are hot springs ===  

{{ill|Higashi Onsen|ja|東温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi Onsen|1=Higashi Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11527997}}
[[File:Iwojima_Higashi_onsen.jpg|thumb|Hot spring at *Higashi Onsen* on Satsuma Iōjima, overlooking the East China Sea]]
Some '''coastal tide pools''' act as natural '''hot springs'''. They are submerged during '''high tide''' and only accessible during '''low tide'''. In some cases, '''waves have carved caves into rocky cliffs''', forming hidden hot spring pools.

Examples:  
- {{ill|Jinata Onsen|ja|地鉈温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinata Onsen|1=Jinata Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11424716}}  
- {{ill|Mizunashi Kaigan Onsen|ja|水無海浜温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizunashi Kaigan Onsen|1=Mizunashi-Kaihin Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11548954}}  

== Anthropogenic discharge ==  

Sometimes, '''construction projects accidentally uncover hot springs''', such as '''roadwork''' or '''foundation digging''' that breaches underground thermal sources. In some cases, municipalities construct '''public bathing areas''' beside these sites, but in others, the water is left to '''flow freely''', forming *noyu* that bathers can use. Some abandoned '''mining sites''' also feature hot springs that emerged from excavation activities.

Examples:  
- {{ill|Kinka-yu|ja|金花湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinka-yu|1=Kinkatou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11647826}}  
- {{ill|Oku-oku Hachikuro Onsen|ja|奥奥八九郎温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oku-oku Hachikuro Onsen|1=Oku-oku Hachikuro Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17224546}}  

== A place that was once a hot spring resort ==  

These were once '''established hot spring resorts''', but due to factors such as '''poor accessibility''' or '''economic decline''', they were abandoned. Some still have intact '''bathtubs''', making them '''usable as wild hot springs'''.

Examples:  
- {{ill|Kōso Onsen|ja|香草温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōso Onsen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}  
- {{ill|Goshiki-yu Ruins|ja|五色湯跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goshiki-yu Ruins|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}  
- {{ill|Kawakita Onsen|ja|川北温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawakita Onsen|1=Kawakita Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11477827}}  
- {{ill|Mitata Onsen|ja|見立温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mitata Onsen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}  

== Use of well-maintained hot springs ==  

Some *noyu* '''reuse discarded thermal water''' from developed '''hot spring resorts'''. In many cases, volunteers '''construct informal bathing facilities''' at these sites. In the past, '''Matsuzaki Onsen''' had an open-air bath created beneath a spring well that collected overflowed hot water. 

Examples:  
- {{ill|Yunotani Onsen|ja|湯の谷温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yunotani Onsen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}  
- {{ill|Kawazu-hama Onsen|ja|河津浜温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawazu-hama Onsen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}

[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:22, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{afc comment|1=Here is an attempt

* 野湯 (Wilderness Hot Springs):  
  These are naturally occurring, undeveloped hot springs found “in the wild.” They lack formal commercial facilities and often require visitors to set up their own bathing arrangements or rely on minimal local upkeep.

* 秘湯 (Secret Hot Springs):  
  Coined around the mid‑1970s, this term refers to hot springs tucked away in remote, hard-to-reach areas (often deep in the mountains). They evoke an aura of mystery and tradition, appealing to those seeking a quiet, off‑the‑beaten‑path retreat.

* 隠し湯 (Hidden Hot Springs):  
  Historically linked to feudal times, these were hot springs that local lords or elites kept exclusively for their own use, deliberately concealed from the public. Today, the term emphasizes their exclusive, almost “forbidden” allure.

* 引湯 (Drawn‑off Hot Springs):  
  Unlike the others that are used at the site of emergence, drawn‑off hot springs involve hot water being piped from its natural source to another location. This process often cools the water slightly and is used to distribute onsen water to developed bathing facilities.

Each term reflects a different aspect—whether it’s the natural setting, historical secrecy, or the method of utilization.

[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:59, 25 March 2025 (UTC)}}

Examples: {{ill|Nakadake Onsen|ja|中岳温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakadake Onsen|qq=}}

=== A hot spring gushes forth from the riverbank ===  

[[File:Hotspring_Kawayu02.jpg|right|thumb|{{ill|Kawayu Onsen (Wakayama)|ja|川湯温泉 (和歌山県)|lt=Kawayu Onsen|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawayu Onsen (Wakayama)|1=Kawayu onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11478936}} Senninburo, with a bath visible across the river]]
In these cases, digging into a '''riverbed''' releases hot spring water. This type allows for '''easy temperature control''' using river water, making it a popular form of *noyu*.

Examples:  
- {{ill|Kawayu Onsen (Wakayama)|ja|川湯温泉 (和歌山県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawayu Onsen (Wakayama)|1=Kawayu onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11478936}}  
- {{ill|Kamiyu Onsen|ja|上湯温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamiyu Onsen|1=Kamiyu Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11359605}}  
- {{ill|Kiriake Onsen|ja|切明温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiriake Onsen|1=Kiriake Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11396212}}  

=== The rivers and lakes themselves are hot springs ===  

This category includes '''rivers and lakes where the bottom serves as a hot spring source'''. If the water is sufficiently hot or the spring has a large flow rate, the entire body of water can become '''suitable for bathing'''.

Examples:  
- {{ill|Kawarage Ōyu Falls|ja|川原毛大湯滝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawarage Ōyu Falls|1=Kawarageoyu Falls|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11477876}}  
- {{ill|Kamuiwakka Falls|ja|カムイワッカの滝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamuiwakka Falls|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カムイワッカ湯の滝|1=Kamuiwakka Falls|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6359812}}  
- {{ill|Ikenoyu (Teshikaga Town)|ja|池の湯 (弟子屈町)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikenoyu (Teshikaga Town)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}  
- {{ill|Yunokawa (Noboribetsu Onsen)|ja|登別温泉 湯の川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yunokawa (Noboribetsu Onsen)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}  
- {{ill|Iōtorino-yu|ja|秋田焼山 硫黄取りの湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iōtorino-yu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}  
- {{ill|Shiriyaki Onsen|ja|尻焼温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiriyaki Onsen|1=Shiriyaki Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11464890}}  
- {{ill|Shinyu (Tateyama Caldera)|ja|立山カルデラ 新湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinyu (Tateyama Caldera)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}  

=== The tide pools on the coast are hot springs ===  

{{ill|Higashi Onsen|ja|東温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi Onsen|1=Higashi Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11527997}}
[[File:Iwojima_Higashi_onsen.jpg|thumb|Hot spring at *Higashi Onsen* on Satsuma Iōjima, overlooking the East China Sea]]
Some '''coastal tide pools''' act as natural '''hot springs'''. They are submerged during '''high tide''' and only accessible during '''low tide'''. In some cases, '''waves have carved caves into rocky cliffs''', forming hidden hot spring pools.

Examples:  
- {{ill|Jinata Onsen|ja|地鉈温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinata Onsen|1=Jinata Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11424716}}  
- {{ill|Mizunashi Kaigan Onsen|ja|水無海浜温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizunashi Kaigan Onsen|1=Mizunashi-Kaihin Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11548954}}  

== Anthropogenic discharge ==  

Sometimes, '''construction projects accidentally uncover hot springs''', such as '''roadwork''' or '''foundation digging''' that breaches underground thermal sources. In some cases, municipalities construct '''public bathing areas''' beside these sites, but in others, the water is left to '''flow freely''', forming *noyu* that bathers can use. Some abandoned '''mining sites''' also feature hot springs that emerged from excavation activities.

Examples:  
- {{ill|Kinka-yu|ja|金花湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinka-yu|1=Kinkatou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11647826}}  
- {{ill|Oku-oku Hachikuro Onsen|ja|奥奥八九郎温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oku-oku Hachikuro Onsen|1=Oku-oku Hachikuro Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17224546}}  

== A place that was once a hot spring resort ==  

These were once '''established hot spring resorts''', but due to factors such as '''poor accessibility''' or '''economic decline''', they were abandoned. Some still have intact '''bathtubs''', making them '''usable as wild hot springs'''.

Examples:  
- {{ill|Kōso Onsen|ja|香草温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōso Onsen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}  
- {{ill|Goshiki-yu Ruins|ja|五色湯跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goshiki-yu Ruins|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}  
- {{ill|Kawakita Onsen|ja|川北温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawakita Onsen|1=Kawakita Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11477827}}  
- {{ill|Mitata Onsen|ja|見立温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mitata Onsen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}  

== Use of well-maintained hot springs ==  

Some *noyu* '''reuse discarded thermal water''' from developed '''hot spring resorts'''. In many cases, volunteers '''construct informal bathing facilities''' at these sites. In the past, '''Matsuzaki Onsen''' had an open-air bath created beneath a spring well that collected overflowed hot water. 

Examples:  
- {{ill|Yunotani Onsen|ja|湯の谷温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yunotani Onsen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}  
- {{ill|Kawazu-hama Onsen|ja|河津浜温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawazu-hama Onsen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}  

{{ill|Onsen|en|Onsen|WD=Q655311|lt=Onsen}}  

{{Onsen}}

Some abandoned hot springs resulted from '''dam construction''', leading to hotels being demolished or collapsing over time. While bath structures may still remain, they are often '''maintained by volunteers'''. Some are '''permanently lost''' when the dam is completed, while others become '''seasonally inaccessible''' during high water levels. Management and ownership have typically transferred to the '''prefecture or national government''', and visitors must follow posted regulations. Generally, existing facilities are '''tolerated''', but constructing new structures is strictly prohibited.
--><!--Wild Hot Springs--><!--Wild Hot Springs-->

Wikidata name: wild onsen
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:22, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{afc comment|1=Here is an attempt

* 野湯 (Wilderness Hot Springs):  
  These are naturally occurring, undeveloped hot springs found “in the wild.” They lack formal commercial facilities and often require visitors to set up their own bathing arrangements or rely on minimal local upkeep.

* 秘湯 (Secret Hot Springs):  
  Coined around the mid‑1970s, this term refers to hot springs tucked away in remote, hard-to-reach areas (often deep in the mountains). They evoke an aura of mystery and tradition, appealing to those seeking a quiet, off‑the‑beaten‑path retreat.

* 隠し湯 (Hidden Hot Springs):  
  Historically linked to feudal times, these were hot springs that local lords or elites kept exclusively for their own use, deliberately concealed from the public. Today, the term emphasizes their exclusive, almost “forbidden” allure.

* 引湯 (Drawn‑off Hot Springs):  
  Unlike the others that are used at the site of emergence, drawn‑off hot springs involve hot water being piped from its natural source to another location. This process often cools the water slightly and is used to distribute onsen water to developed bathing facilities.

Each term reflects a different aspect—whether it’s the natural setting, historical secrecy, or the method of utilization.

[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:59, 25 March 2025 (UTC)}}
{{afc comment|1=Need to figure out how to properly differentiate these four types
*{{ill|Wild hot spring|ja|野湯|1=Wild Hot Springs|1=Wild Hot Springs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wild hot spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Wild Hot Springs|qq=}}
*{{ill|Secret hot spring|ja|秘湯|1=Secret hot spring|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Secret hot spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Secret hot spring|qq=}}
*{{ill|Hidden hot spring|ja|隠し湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hidden hot spring|qq=}}
*{{ill|Drawn‐off hot spring|ja|引湯|1=Drawn‐off hot spring|2=ja|3=引湯|lt=Drawn‐off hot spring|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Drawn‐off hot spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Drawn‐off hot spring|qq=}}
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:50, 25 March 2025 (UTC)}}
{{More citations needed}}
'''Noyu''' (野湯) refers to '''hot springs''' that emerge '''naturally in the wild''' without any commercial facilities built around their source. These hot springs exist in various forms—some are maintained regularly by local community associations or enthusiasts, while others require visitors to bring materials such as sheets, sandbags, or shovels to prepare a bathing spot. Some *noyu* pose life-threatening risks due to volcanic gas emissions like '''hydrogen sulfide''', while others are off-limits for reasons such as '''environmental protection''' or '''private ownership restrictions'''.

== Natural hot springs flowing from the source ==  

[[File:Nakadake_hotspring_daisetuzan01.jpg|right|thumb|{{ill|Nakadake Onsen|ja|中岳温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakadake Onsen|qq=}} open-air bath, with a maintenance shovel visible]]
The most common form of *noyu* involves hot springs naturally emerging along riverbanks or mountain slopes. In dormant and active volcanoes, groundwater is heated by magma and erupts to form hot spring rivers or even entire '''thermal lakes'''.

=== The hot spring gushes forth from the mountainside ===  

This type occurs when '''hot spring sources seep from mountain slopes''' or, in geothermal areas, '''multiple springs burst out of the ground'''. These sites often lack built-in bathing facilities, requiring visitors to dig holes in the ground or use portable plastic pools or sheets to create makeshift baths. In some cases, volunteers construct basic bath structures, but {{Citation needed|some of these may have been built without landowner permission or illegally within national parks.|date=January 2011}}. Temperature control is often difficult.

Examples: {{ill|Nakadake Onsen|ja|中岳温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakadake Onsen|qq=}}

=== A hot spring gushes forth from the riverbank ===  

[[File:Hotspring_Kawayu02.jpg|right|thumb|{{ill|Kawayu Onsen (Wakayama)|ja|川湯温泉 (和歌山県)|lt=Kawayu Onsen|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawayu Onsen (Wakayama)|1=Kawayu onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11478936}} Senninburo, with a bath visible across the river]]
In these cases, digging into a '''riverbed''' releases hot spring water. This type allows for '''easy temperature control''' using river water, making it a popular form of *noyu*.

Examples:  
- {{ill|Kawayu Onsen (Wakayama)|ja|川湯温泉 (和歌山県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawayu Onsen (Wakayama)|1=Kawayu onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11478936}}  
- {{ill|Kamiyu Onsen|ja|上湯温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamiyu Onsen|1=Kamiyu Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11359605}}  
- {{ill|Kiriake Onsen|ja|切明温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiriake Onsen|1=Kiriake Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11396212}}  

=== The rivers and lakes themselves are hot springs ===  

This category includes '''rivers and lakes where the bottom serves as a hot spring source'''. If the water is sufficiently hot or the spring has a large flow rate, the entire body of water can become '''suitable for bathing'''.

Examples:  
- {{ill|Kawarage Ōyu Falls|ja|川原毛大湯滝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawarage Ōyu Falls|1=Kawarageoyu Falls|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11477876}}  
- {{ill|Kamuiwakka Falls|ja|カムイワッカの滝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamuiwakka Falls|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カムイワッカ湯の滝|1=Kamuiwakka Falls|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6359812}}  
- {{ill|Ikenoyu (Teshikaga Town)|ja|池の湯 (弟子屈町)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikenoyu (Teshikaga Town)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}  
- {{ill|Yunokawa (Noboribetsu Onsen)|ja|登別温泉 湯の川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yunokawa (Noboribetsu Onsen)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}  
- {{ill|Iōtorino-yu|ja|秋田焼山 硫黄取りの湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iōtorino-yu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}  
- {{ill|Shiriyaki Onsen|ja|尻焼温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiriyaki Onsen|1=Shiriyaki Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11464890}}  
- {{ill|Shinyu (Tateyama Caldera)|ja|立山カルデラ 新湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinyu (Tateyama Caldera)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}  

=== The tide pools on the coast are hot springs ===  

{{ill|Higashi Onsen|ja|東温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi Onsen|1=Higashi Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11527997}}
[[File:Iwojima_Higashi_onsen.jpg|thumb|Hot spring at *Higashi Onsen* on Satsuma Iōjima, overlooking the East China Sea]]
Some '''coastal tide pools''' act as natural '''hot springs'''. They are submerged during '''high tide''' and only accessible during '''low tide'''. In some cases, '''waves have carved caves into rocky cliffs''', forming hidden hot spring pools.

Examples:  
- {{ill|Jinata Onsen|ja|地鉈温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinata Onsen|1=Jinata Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11424716}}  
- {{ill|Mizunashi Kaigan Onsen|ja|水無海浜温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizunashi Kaigan Onsen|1=Mizunashi-Kaihin Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11548954}}  

== Anthropogenic discharge ==  

Sometimes, '''construction projects accidentally uncover hot springs''', such as '''roadwork''' or '''foundation digging''' that breaches underground thermal sources. In some cases, municipalities construct '''public bathing areas''' beside these sites, but in others, the water is left to '''flow freely''', forming *noyu* that bathers can use. Some abandoned '''mining sites''' also feature hot springs that emerged from excavation activities.

Examples:  
- {{ill|Kinka-yu|ja|金花湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinka-yu|1=Kinkatou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11647826}}  
- {{ill|Oku-oku Hachikuro Onsen|ja|奥奥八九郎温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oku-oku Hachikuro Onsen|1=Oku-oku Hachikuro Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17224546}}  

== A place that was once a hot spring resort ==  

These were once '''established hot spring resorts''', but due to factors such as '''poor accessibility''' or '''economic decline''', they were abandoned. Some still have intact '''bathtubs''', making them '''usable as wild hot springs'''.

Examples:  
- {{ill|Kōso Onsen|ja|香草温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōso Onsen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}  
- {{ill|Goshiki-yu Ruins|ja|五色湯跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goshiki-yu Ruins|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}  
- {{ill|Kawakita Onsen|ja|川北温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawakita Onsen|1=Kawakita Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11477827}}  
- {{ill|Mitata Onsen|ja|見立温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mitata Onsen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}  

== Use of well-maintained hot springs ==  

Some *noyu* '''reuse discarded thermal water''' from developed '''hot spring resorts'''. In many cases, volunteers '''construct informal bathing facilities''' at these sites. In the past, '''Matsuzaki Onsen''' had an open-air bath created beneath a spring well that collected overflowed hot water. 

Examples:  
- {{ill|Yunotani Onsen|ja|湯の谷温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yunotani Onsen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}  
- {{ill|Kawazu-hama Onsen|ja|河津浜温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawazu-hama Onsen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}

- {{ill|Kawarage Ōyu Falls|ja|川原毛大湯滝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawarage Ōyu Falls|1=Kawarageoyu Falls|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11477876}}  
- {{ill|Kamuiwakka Falls|ja|カムイワッカの滝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamuiwakka Falls|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カムイワッカ湯の滝|1=Kamuiwakka Falls|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6359812}}  
- {{ill|Ikenoyu (Teshikaga Town)|ja|池の湯 (弟子屈町)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikenoyu (Teshikaga Town)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}  
- {{ill|Yunokawa (Noboribetsu Onsen)|ja|登別温泉 湯の川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yunokawa (Noboribetsu Onsen)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}  
- {{ill|Iōtorino-yu|ja|秋田焼山 硫黄取りの湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iōtorino-yu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}  
- {{ill|Shiriyaki Onsen|ja|尻焼温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiriyaki Onsen|1=Shiriyaki Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11464890}}  
- {{ill|Shinyu (Tateyama Caldera)|ja|立山カルデラ 新湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinyu (Tateyama Caldera)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}  

=== The tide pools on the coast are hot springs ===  

{{ill|Higashi Onsen|ja|東温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi Onsen|1=Higashi Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11527997}}
[[File:Iwojima_Higashi_onsen.jpg|thumb|Hot spring at *Higashi Onsen* on Satsuma Iōjima, overlooking the East China Sea]]
Some '''coastal tide pools''' act as natural '''hot springs'''. They are submerged during '''high tide''' and only accessible during '''low tide'''. In some cases, '''waves have carved caves into rocky cliffs''', forming hidden hot spring pools.

Examples:  
- {{ill|Jinata Onsen|ja|地鉈温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinata Onsen|1=Jinata Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11424716}}  
- {{ill|Mizunashi Kaigan Onsen|ja|水無海浜温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizunashi Kaigan Onsen|1=Mizunashi-Kaihin Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11548954}}  

== Anthropogenic discharge ==  

Sometimes, '''construction projects accidentally uncover hot springs''', such as '''roadwork''' or '''foundation digging''' that breaches underground thermal sources. In some cases, municipalities construct '''public bathing areas''' beside these sites, but in others, the water is left to '''flow freely''', forming *noyu* that bathers can use. Some abandoned '''mining sites''' also feature hot springs that emerged from excavation activities.

Examples:  
- {{ill|Kinka-yu|ja|金花湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinka-yu|1=Kinkatou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11647826}}  
- {{ill|Oku-oku Hachikuro Onsen|ja|奥奥八九郎温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oku-oku Hachikuro Onsen|1=Oku-oku Hachikuro Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17224546}}  

== A place that was once a hot spring resort ==  

These were once '''established hot spring resorts''', but due to factors such as '''poor accessibility''' or '''economic decline''', they were abandoned. Some still have intact '''bathtubs''', making them '''usable as wild hot springs'''.

Examples:  
- {{ill|Kōso Onsen|ja|香草温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōso Onsen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}  
- {{ill|Goshiki-yu Ruins|ja|五色湯跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goshiki-yu Ruins|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}  
- {{ill|Kawakita Onsen|ja|川北温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawakita Onsen|1=Kawakita Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11477827}}  
- {{ill|Mitata Onsen|ja|見立温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mitata Onsen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}  

== Use of well-maintained hot springs ==  

Some *noyu* '''reuse discarded thermal water''' from developed '''hot spring resorts'''. In many cases, volunteers '''construct informal bathing facilities''' at these sites. In the past, '''Matsuzaki Onsen''' had an open-air bath created beneath a spring well that collected overflowed hot water. 

Examples:  
- {{ill|Yunotani Onsen|ja|湯の谷温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yunotani Onsen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}  
- {{ill|Kawazu-hama Onsen|ja|河津浜温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawazu-hama Onsen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}  

{{ill|Onsen|en|Onsen|WD=Q655311|lt=Onsen}}

Some abandoned hot springs resulted from '''dam construction''', leading to hotels being demolished or collapsing over time. While bath structures may still remain, they are often '''maintained by volunteers'''. Some are '''permanently lost''' when the dam is completed, while others become '''seasonally inaccessible''' during high water levels. Management and ownership have typically transferred to the '''prefecture or national government''', and visitors must follow posted regulations. Generally, existing facilities are '''tolerated''', but constructing new structures is strictly prohibited.
--><!--Wild Hot Springs--><!--Wild Hot Springs-->

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:野湯]]



*{{ill|Secret hot spring|ja|秘湯|1=Secret hot spring|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Secret hot spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Secret hot spring|qq=}}
*{{ill|Hidden hot spring|ja|隠し湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hidden hot spring|qq=}}
*{{ill|Drawn‐off hot spring|ja|引湯|1=Drawn‐off hot spring|2=ja|3=引湯|lt=Drawn‐off hot spring|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Drawn‐off hot spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Drawn‐off hot spring|qq=}}
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:50, 25 March 2025 (UTC)}}
{{More citations needed}}
'''Noyu''' (野湯) refers to '''hot springs''' that emerge '''naturally in the wild''' without any commercial facilities built around their source. These hot springs exist in various forms—some are maintained regularly by local community associations or enthusiasts, while others require visitors to bring materials such as sheets, sandbags, or shovels to prepare a bathing spot. Some *noyu* pose life-threatening risks due to volcanic gas emissions like '''hydrogen sulfide''', while others are off-limits for reasons such as '''environmental protection''' or '''private ownership restrictions'''.

== Natural hot springs flowing from the source ==  

[[File:Nakadake_hotspring_daisetuzan01.jpg|right|thumb|{{ill|Nakadake Onsen|ja|中岳温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakadake Onsen|qq=}} open-air bath, with a maintenance shovel visible]]
The most common form of *noyu* involves hot springs naturally emerging along riverbanks or mountain slopes. In dormant and active volcanoes, groundwater is heated by magma and erupts to form hot spring rivers or even entire '''thermal lakes'''.

=== The hot spring gushes forth from the mountainside ===  

This type occurs when '''hot spring sources seep from mountain slopes''' or, in geothermal areas, '''multiple springs burst out of the ground'''. These sites often lack built-in bathing facilities, requiring visitors to dig holes in the ground or use portable plastic pools or sheets to create makeshift baths. In some cases, volunteers construct basic bath structures, but {{Citation needed|some of these may have been built without landowner permission or illegally within national parks.|date=January 2011}}. Temperature control is often difficult.

Examples: {{ill|Nakadake Onsen|ja|中岳温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakadake Onsen|qq=}}

=== A hot spring gushes forth from the riverbank ===  

[[File:Hotspring_Kawayu02.jpg|right|thumb|{{ill|Kawayu Onsen (Wakayama)|ja|川湯温泉 (和歌山県)|lt=Kawayu Onsen|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawayu Onsen (Wakayama)|1=Kawayu onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11478936}} Senninburo, with a bath visible across the river]]
In these cases, digging into a '''riverbed''' releases hot spring water. This type allows for '''easy temperature control''' using river water, making it a popular form of *noyu*.

Examples:  
- {{ill|Kawayu Onsen (Wakayama)|ja|川湯温泉 (和歌山県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawayu Onsen (Wakayama)|1=Kawayu onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11478936}}  
- {{ill|Kamiyu Onsen|ja|上湯温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamiyu Onsen|1=Kamiyu Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11359605}}  
- {{ill|Kiriake Onsen|ja|切明温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiriake Onsen|1=Kiriake Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11396212}}  

=== The rivers and lakes themselves are hot springs ===  

This category includes '''rivers and lakes where the bottom serves as a hot spring source'''. If the water is sufficiently hot or the spring has a large flow rate, the entire body of water can become '''suitable for bathing'''.

Examples:  
- {{ill|Kawarage Ōyu Falls|ja|川原毛大湯滝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawarage Ōyu Falls|1=Kawarageoyu Falls|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11477876}}  
- {{ill|Kamuiwakka Falls|ja|カムイワッカの滝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamuiwakka Falls|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カムイワッカ湯の滝|1=Kamuiwakka Falls|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6359812}}  
- {{ill|Ikenoyu (Teshikaga Town)|ja|池の湯 (弟子屈町)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikenoyu (Teshikaga Town)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}  
- {{ill|Yunokawa (Noboribetsu Onsen)|ja|登別温泉 湯の川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yunokawa (Noboribetsu Onsen)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}  
- {{ill|Iōtorino-yu|ja|秋田焼山 硫黄取りの湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iōtorino-yu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}  
- {{ill|Shiriyaki Onsen|ja|尻焼温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiriyaki Onsen|1=Shiriyaki Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11464890}}  
- {{ill|Shinyu (Tateyama Caldera)|ja|立山カルデラ 新湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinyu (Tateyama Caldera)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}  

=== The tide pools on the coast are hot springs ===  

{{ill|Higashi Onsen|ja|東温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi Onsen|1=Higashi Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11527997}}
[[File:Iwojima_Higashi_onsen.jpg|thumb|Hot spring at *Higashi Onsen* on Satsuma Iōjima, overlooking the East China Sea]]
Some '''coastal tide pools''' act as natural '''hot springs'''. They are submerged during '''high tide''' and only accessible during '''low tide'''. In some cases, '''waves have carved caves into rocky cliffs''', forming hidden hot spring pools.

Examples:  
- {{ill|Jinata Onsen|ja|地鉈温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinata Onsen|1=Jinata Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11424716}}  
- {{ill|Mizunashi Kaigan Onsen|ja|水無海浜温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizunashi Kaigan Onsen|1=Mizunashi-Kaihin Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11548954}}  

== Anthropogenic discharge ==  

Sometimes, '''construction projects accidentally uncover hot springs''', such as '''roadwork''' or '''foundation digging''' that breaches underground thermal sources. In some cases, municipalities construct '''public bathing areas''' beside these sites, but in others, the water is left to '''flow freely''', forming *noyu* that bathers can use. Some abandoned '''mining sites''' also feature hot springs that emerged from excavation activities.

Examples:  
- {{ill|Kinka-yu|ja|金花湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinka-yu|1=Kinkatou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11647826}}  
- {{ill|Oku-oku Hachikuro Onsen|ja|奥奥八九郎温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oku-oku Hachikuro Onsen|1=Oku-oku Hachikuro Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17224546}}  

== A place that was once a hot spring resort ==  

These were once '''established hot spring resorts''', but due to factors such as '''poor accessibility''' or '''economic decline''', they were abandoned. Some still have intact '''bathtubs''', making them '''usable as wild hot springs'''.

Examples:  
- {{ill|Kōso Onsen|ja|香草温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōso Onsen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}  
- {{ill|Goshiki-yu Ruins|ja|五色湯跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goshiki-yu Ruins|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}  
- {{ill|Kawakita Onsen|ja|川北温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawakita Onsen|1=Kawakita Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11477827}}  
- {{ill|Mitata Onsen|ja|見立温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mitata Onsen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}  

== Use of well-maintained hot springs ==  

Some *noyu* '''reuse discarded thermal water''' from developed '''hot spring resorts'''. In many cases, volunteers '''construct informal bathing facilities''' at these sites. In the past, '''Matsuzaki Onsen''' had an open-air bath created beneath a spring well that collected overflowed hot water. 

Examples:  
- {{ill|Yunotani Onsen|ja|湯の谷温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yunotani Onsen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}  
- {{ill|Kawazu-hama Onsen|ja|河津浜温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawazu-hama Onsen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:野湯]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:野湯]]



*{{ill|Secret hot spring|ja|秘湯|1=Secret hot spring|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Secret hot spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Secret hot spring|qq=}}
*{{ill|Hidden hot spring|ja|隠し湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hidden hot spring|qq=}}
*{{ill|Drawn‐off hot spring|ja|引湯|1=Drawn‐off hot spring|2=ja|3=引湯|lt=Drawn‐off hot spring|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Drawn‐off hot spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Drawn‐off hot spring|qq=}}
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:50, 25 March 2025 (UTC)}}
{{More citations needed}}
'''Noyu''' (野湯) refers to '''hot springs''' that emerge '''naturally in the wild''' without any commercial facilities built around their source. These hot springs exist in various forms—some are maintained regularly by local community associations or enthusiasts, while others require visitors to bring materials such as sheets, sandbags, or shovels to prepare a bathing spot. Some *noyu* pose life-threatening risks due to volcanic gas emissions like '''hydrogen sulfide''', while others are off-limits for reasons such as '''environmental protection''' or '''private ownership restrictions'''.

== Natural hot springs flowing from the source ==  

[[File:Nakadake_hotspring_daisetuzan01.jpg|right|thumb|{{ill|Nakadake Onsen|ja|中岳温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakadake Onsen|qq=}} open-air bath, with a maintenance shovel visible]]
The most common form of *noyu* involves hot springs naturally emerging along riverbanks or mountain slopes. In dormant and active volcanoes, groundwater is heated by magma and erupts to form hot spring rivers or even entire '''thermal lakes'''.

=== The hot spring gushes forth from the mountainside ===  

This type occurs when '''hot spring sources seep from mountain slopes''' or, in geothermal areas, '''multiple springs burst out of the ground'''. These sites often lack built-in bathing facilities, requiring visitors to dig holes in the ground or use portable plastic pools or sheets to create makeshift baths. In some cases, volunteers construct basic bath structures, but {{Citation needed|some of these may have been built without landowner permission or illegally within national parks.|date=January 2011}}. Temperature control is often difficult.

Examples: {{ill|Nakadake Onsen|ja|中岳温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakadake Onsen|qq=}}

=== A hot spring gushes forth from the riverbank ===  

[[File:Hotspring_Kawayu02.jpg|right|thumb|{{ill|Kawayu Onsen (Wakayama)|ja|川湯温泉 (和歌山県)|lt=Kawayu Onsen|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawayu Onsen (Wakayama)|1=Kawayu onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11478936}} Senninburo, with a bath visible across the river]]
In these cases, digging into a '''riverbed''' releases hot spring water. This type allows for '''easy temperature control''' using river water, making it a popular form of *noyu*.

Examples:  
- {{ill|Kawayu Onsen (Wakayama)|ja|川湯温泉 (和歌山県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawayu Onsen (Wakayama)|1=Kawayu onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11478936}}  
- {{ill|Kamiyu Onsen|ja|上湯温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamiyu Onsen|1=Kami-Yu Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11359605}}  
- {{ill|Kiriake Onsen|ja|切明温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiriake Onsen|1=Kiriake Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11396212}}  

=== The rivers and lakes themselves are hot springs ===  

This category includes '''rivers and lakes where the bottom serves as a hot spring source'''. If the water is sufficiently hot or the spring has a large flow rate, the entire body of water can become '''suitable for bathing'''.

Examples:  
- {{ill|Kawarage Ōyu Falls|ja|川原毛大湯滝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawarage Ōyu Falls|1=Kawarageoyu Falls|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11477876}}  
- {{ill|Kamuiwakka Falls|ja|カムイワッカの滝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamuiwakka Falls|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カムイワッカ湯の滝|1=Kamuiwakka Falls|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6359812}}  
- {{ill|Ikenoyu (Teshikaga Town)|ja|池の湯 (弟子屈町)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikenoyu (Teshikaga Town)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}  
- {{ill|Yunokawa (Noboribetsu Onsen)|ja|登別温泉 湯の川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yunokawa (Noboribetsu Onsen)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}  
- {{ill|Iōtorino-yu|ja|秋田焼山 硫黄取りの湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iōtorino-yu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}  
- {{ill|Shiriyaki Onsen|ja|尻焼温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiriyaki Onsen|1=Shiriyaki Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11464890}}  
- {{ill|Shinyu (Tateyama Caldera)|ja|立山カルデラ 新湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinyu (Tateyama Caldera)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}  

=== The tide pools on the coast are hot springs ===  

{{ill|Higashi Onsen|ja|東温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi Onsen|1=Higashi Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11527997}}
[[File:Iwojima_Higashi_onsen.jpg|thumb|Hot spring at *Higashi Onsen* on Satsuma Iōjima, overlooking the East China Sea]]
Some '''coastal tide pools''' act as natural '''hot springs'''. They are submerged during '''high tide''' and only accessible during '''low tide'''. In some cases, '''waves have carved caves into rocky cliffs''', forming hidden hot spring pools.

Examples:  
- {{ill|Jinata Onsen|ja|地鉈温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinata Onsen|1=Jinata Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11424716}}  
- {{ill|Mizunashi Kaigan Onsen|ja|水無海浜温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizunashi Kaigan Onsen|1=Mizunashi-Kaihin Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11548954}}  

== Anthropogenic discharge ==  

Sometimes, '''construction projects accidentally uncover hot springs''', such as '''roadwork''' or '''foundation digging''' that breaches underground thermal sources. In some cases, municipalities construct '''public bathing areas''' beside these sites, but in others, the water is left to '''flow freely''', forming *noyu* that bathers can use. Some abandoned '''mining sites''' also feature hot springs that emerged from excavation activities.

Examples:  
- {{ill|Kinka-yu|ja|金花湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinka-yu|1=Kinkatou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11647826}}  
- {{ill|Oku-oku Hachikuro Onsen|ja|奥奥八九郎温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oku-oku Hachikuro Onsen|1=Oku-oku Hachikuro Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17224546}}  

== A place that was once a hot spring resort ==  

These were once '''established hot spring resorts''', but due to factors such as '''poor accessibility''' or '''economic decline''', they were abandoned. Some still have intact '''bathtubs''', making them '''usable as wild hot springs'''.

Examples:  
- {{ill|Kōso Onsen|ja|香草温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōso Onsen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}  
- {{ill|Goshiki-yu Ruins|ja|五色湯跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goshiki-yu Ruins|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}  
- {{ill|Kawakita Onsen|ja|川北温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawakita Onsen|1=Q11477827|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11477827}}  
- {{ill|Mitata Onsen|ja|見立温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mitata Onsen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}  

== Use of well-maintained hot springs ==  

Some *noyu* '''reuse discarded thermal water''' from developed '''hot spring resorts'''. In many cases, volunteers '''construct informal bathing facilities''' at these sites. In the past, '''Matsuzaki Onsen''' had an open-air bath created beneath a spring well that collected overflowed hot water. 

Examples:  
- {{ill|Yunotani Onsen|ja|湯の谷温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yunotani Onsen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}  
- {{ill|Kawazu-hama Onsen|ja|河津浜温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawazu-hama Onsen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}  

{{ill|Onsen|en|Onsen|WD=Q655311|lt=Onsen|1=Onsen}}  

{{Onsen}}

{{translated page|ja|野湯|version=96866729|comment=Imported full ja history}}



[[ja:野湯]]

Some abandoned hot springs resulted from '''dam construction''', leading to hotels being demolished or collapsing over time. While bath structures may still remain, they are often '''maintained by volunteers'''. Some are '''permanently lost''' when the dam is completed, while others become '''seasonally inaccessible''' during high water levels. Management and ownership have typically transferred to the '''prefecture or national government''', and visitors must follow posted regulations. Generally, existing facilities are '''tolerated''', but constructing new structures is strictly prohibited.
--><!--Wild Hot Springs--><!--Wild Hot Springs-->

=== The tide pools on the coast are hot springs ===  

{{ill|Higashi Onsen|ja|東温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi Onsen|1=Higashi Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11527997}}
[[File:Iwojima_Higashi_onsen.jpg|thumb|Hot spring at *Higashi Onsen* on Satsuma Iōjima, overlooking the East China Sea]]
Some '''coastal tide pools''' act as natural '''hot springs'''. They are submerged during '''high tide''' and only accessible during '''low tide'''. In some cases, '''waves have carved caves into rocky cliffs''', forming hidden hot spring pools.

Examples:  
- {{ill|Jinata Onsen|ja|地鉈温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinata Onsen|1=Jinata Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11424716}}  
- {{ill|Mizunashi Kaigan Onsen|ja|水無海浜温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizunashi Kaigan Onsen|1=Mizunashi-Kaihin Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11548954}}  

== Anthropogenic discharge ==  

Sometimes, '''construction projects accidentally uncover hot springs''', such as '''roadwork''' or '''foundation digging''' that breaches underground thermal sources. In some cases, municipalities construct '''public bathing areas''' beside these sites, but in others, the water is left to '''flow freely''', forming *noyu* that bathers can use. Some abandoned '''mining sites''' also feature hot springs that emerged from excavation activities.

Examples:  
- {{ill|Kinka-yu|ja|金花湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinka-yu|1=Kinkatou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11647826}}  
- {{ill|Oku-oku Hachikuro Onsen|ja|奥奥八九郎温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oku-oku Hachikuro Onsen|1=Oku-oku Hachikuro Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17224546}}  

== A place that was once a hot spring resort ==  

These were once '''established hot spring resorts''', but due to factors such as '''poor accessibility''' or '''economic decline''', they were abandoned. Some still have intact '''bathtubs''', making them '''usable as wild hot springs'''.

Examples:  
- {{ill|Kōso Onsen|ja|香草温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōso Onsen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}  
- {{ill|Goshiki-yu Ruins|ja|五色湯跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goshiki-yu Ruins|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}  
- {{ill|Kawakita Onsen|ja|川北温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawakita Onsen|1=Q11477827|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11477827}}  
- {{ill|Mitata Onsen|ja|見立温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mitata Onsen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}  

== Use of well-maintained hot springs ==  

Some *noyu* '''reuse discarded thermal water''' from developed '''hot spring resorts'''. In many cases, volunteers '''construct informal bathing facilities''' at these sites. In the past, '''Matsuzaki Onsen''' had an open-air bath created beneath a spring well that collected overflowed hot water. 

Examples:  
- {{ill|Yunotani Onsen|ja|湯の谷温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yunotani Onsen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}  
- {{ill|Kawazu-hama Onsen|ja|河津浜温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawazu-hama Onsen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}  

{{ill|Onsen|en|Onsen|WD=Q655311|lt=Onsen|1=Onsen}}

Some abandoned hot springs resulted from '''dam construction''', leading to hotels being demolished or collapsing over time. While bath structures may still remain, they are often '''maintained by volunteers'''. Some are '''permanently lost''' when the dam is completed, while others become '''seasonally inaccessible''' during high water levels. Management and ownership have typically transferred to the '''prefecture or national government''', and visitors must follow posted regulations. Generally, existing facilities are '''tolerated''', but constructing new structures is strictly prohibited.
--><!--Wild Hot Springs--><!--Wild Hot Springs-->
